title: What is Data?
description: Understand what data is and why it matters.
introduction: Before writing SQL, let's understand what data actually is. Everything around you — names, prices, dates — is data waiting to be organized.

sections.title: Data in Everyday Life
sections.content: Data is any piece of information that can be recorded and used. Your name, age, email address, the price of coffee, today's date — all of these are data points.
sections.analogy: Think of data like ingredients in a kitchen. A single tomato is one data point. A recipe card listing tomatoes, basil, and olive oil is a collection of related data.

practice.question: Which of these is NOT data?

hints.title: Think about recording
hints.content: Data can be written down, stored, and compared. Can you write down a feeling as a fact?

hints.title: Common mistake
hints.content: Numbers and text are data. Abstract feelings without measurement are harder to store as data.

hints.title: Answer
hints.content: A feeling of happiness is subjective and not easily recorded as structured data.

practice.solution: SELECT 'A feeling of happiness' AS answer;
practice.explanation: Data must be recordable. Names, ages, and prices can be stored. Feelings are subjective unless measured (like a survey rating).

summary: Data can be numbers, text, dates, or true/false values
Organized data becomes useful for decisions

title: What is a Database?
description: Learn what databases are and how they store information.
introduction: A database is an organized collection of data. Instead of scattered spreadsheets, a database keeps everything structured and searchable.

sections.title: Database vs Spreadsheet
sections.content: A spreadsheet works for small lists. A database handles millions of records, enforces rules (like 'email must be unique'), and lets many users access data safely at the same time.
sections.analogy: A spreadsheet is like a notebook. A database is like a library with a catalog system — organized, searchable, and managed.

summary: SQL is the language to talk to databases

title: Tables, Rows & Columns
description: Master the fundamental building blocks of relational databases.
introduction: Data in a database lives in tables. Think of a table like a spreadsheet with a strict structure.

sections.title: Table Structure
sections.content: A **table** holds data about one type of thing (students, products, orders). **Columns** define what properties each item has (name, age, price). **Rows** are individual records — one complete item.
sections.analogy: A table is like a class attendance sheet. Columns are headers (Name, Roll No, Present). Each row is one student.

practice.question: Write a query to see all columns and all rows from the students table.

hints.title: SELECT and FROM
hints.content: Use SELECT to choose columns and FROM to specify the table. * means all columns.

hints.title: Table name
hints.content: The table is called 'students' (plural). Make sure spelling matches exactly.

hints.title: Expected output
hints.content: You should see 8 rows with 7 columns each.

practice.explanation: SELECT * retrieves every column. FROM students tells SQLite which table to read.

title: Introduction to SQL
description: Learn what SQL is and write your first query.
introduction: SQL (Structured Query Language) is how you communicate with a database. You ask questions, and the database answers with data.

sections.title: Your First Query
sections.content: The most common SQL command is SELECT. It retrieves data from tables. Every query needs at minimum: SELECT (what to show) and FROM (where to get it).

practice.question: Select only the first_name and age columns from students.

hints.title: Specific columns
hints.content: Instead of *, list column names separated by commas after SELECT.

hints.title: Spelling
hints.content: Column names are first_name and age — use underscores, not spaces.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: 8 rows with two columns: first_name and age.

practice.explanation: Listing specific columns after SELECT returns only those fields.

title: SELECT & DISTINCT
description: Retrieve unique values and avoid duplicates.
introduction: Sometimes you only want unique values. DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from your results.

sections.title: Removing Duplicates
sections.content: SELECT DISTINCT column_name returns only unique values. Without DISTINCT, every matching row appears — even duplicates.

practice.question: Get a list of unique departments from the courses table.

hints.title: DISTINCT keyword
hints.content: Add DISTINCT right after SELECT to get unique values.

hints.title: One column
hints.content: You only need the department column.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: 4 unique departments, no duplicates.

practice.explanation: DISTINCT collapses duplicate department names into one row each.

title: Filtering with WHERE
introduction: Not every row is relevant. WHERE lets you filter — like asking 'show me only students older than 20'.

sections.title: Conditions
sections.content: WHERE comes after FROM and filters rows before they're returned. Use =, >, <, >=, <=, != for comparisons. Text values need single quotes: WHERE name = 'Alice'.
sections.analogy: WHERE is like a bouncer at a club — only rows that pass the condition get through.

practice.question: Find all students with a GPA greater than 3.5.

hints.title: Comparison operator
hints.content: Use > for 'greater than'. Numbers don't need quotes.

hints.title: NULL values
hints.content: Students with NULL GPA won't match > 3.5. That's expected.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: Alice (3.8), Carol (3.9), and Grace (3.7) should appear.

practice.explanation: WHERE gpa > 3.5 filters to only rows where the GPA column exceeds 3.5.

title: ORDER BY & LIMIT
description: Sort results and limit how many rows you get.
introduction: ORDER BY sorts your results. LIMIT caps how many rows are returned.

sections.title: Sorting and Limiting
sections.content: ORDER BY column ASC sorts ascending (A→Z, 1→9). DESC sorts descending. LIMIT n returns only the first n rows after sorting.
sections.exampleSql: SELECT * FROM students ORDER BY gpa DESC LIMIT 3;

practice.question: Show the top 3 students by GPA (highest first).

hints.title: Sort direction
hints.content: Use ORDER BY gpa DESC for highest first.

hints.title: Limit rows
hints.content: Add LIMIT 3 at the end.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: Carol (3.9), Alice (3.8), Grace (3.7) in that order.

practice.solution: SELECT * FROM students WHERE gpa IS NOT NULL ORDER BY gpa DESC LIMIT 3;
practice.explanation: ORDER BY gpa DESC sorts highest GPA first. LIMIT 3 keeps only the top 3.

title: LIKE, IN & BETWEEN
introduction: LIKE finds patterns in text. IN checks membership in a list. BETWEEN filters ranges.

sections.title: Pattern Matching
sections.content: LIKE uses % as wildcard: 'A%' matches anything starting with A. IN (1,2,3) matches any value in the list. BETWEEN 10 AND 20 includes both endpoints.
sections.exampleSql: SELECT * FROM students WHERE first_name LIKE 'A%';

practice.question: Find students whose first name starts with 'C' or 'G'.

hints.title: LIKE patterns
hints.content: Use LIKE 'C%' for names starting with C.

hints.title: Multiple conditions
hints.content: Combine with OR: ... LIKE 'C%' OR first_name LIKE 'G%'

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: Carol and Grace should appear.

practice.solution: SELECT * FROM students WHERE first_name LIKE 'C%' OR first_name LIKE 'G%';
practice.explanation: LIKE with % wildcard matches patterns. OR combines multiple conditions.

title: Aggregate Functions
description: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX — summarize data.
introduction: Aggregate functions crunch many rows into a single summary value.

sections.title: Summarizing Data
sections.content: COUNT(*) counts rows. SUM(column) adds values. AVG(column) calculates average. MIN and MAX find extremes.
sections.exampleSql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total, AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa FROM students WHERE gpa IS NOT NULL;

practice.question: How many students are enrolled? Return the count as 'total_students'.

hints.title: COUNT
hints.content: COUNT(*) counts all rows in the table.

hints.title: Alias
hints.content: Use AS total_students to name the result column.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: One row with total_students = 8.

practice.solution: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_students FROM students;
practice.explanation: COUNT(*) returns the total number of rows — 8 students.

summary: Use aliases for readable column names

title: GROUP BY & HAVING
introduction: GROUP BY splits data into groups. HAVING filters those groups after aggregation.

sections.title: Grouping Data
sections.content: GROUP BY column creates one row per unique value. HAVING filters groups (like WHERE but for aggregates).
sections.exampleSql: SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS course_count FROM courses GROUP BY department HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

practice.question: Count courses per department. Show department and course_count.

hints.title: GROUP BY
hints.content: Group by department, count rows in each group.

hints.title: Non-aggregate columns
hints.content: department must appear in GROUP BY since it's in SELECT.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: 4 rows, one per department with counts.

practice.solution: SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS course_count FROM courses GROUP BY department;
practice.explanation: GROUP BY department creates groups. COUNT(*) counts courses in each.

summary: Non-aggregate columns must be in GROUP BY

title: INNER JOIN
introduction: Real data lives across tables. JOINs connect them using shared keys.

sections.title: Joining Tables
sections.content: INNER JOIN returns only rows that match in BOTH tables. The ON clause specifies how tables connect: ON students.student_id = enrollments.student_id.
sections.analogy: A JOIN is like matching puzzle pieces — only pieces that fit together (matching IDs) are kept.
sections.exampleSql: SELECT s.first_name, c.course_name FROM students s INNER JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id INNER JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id;

practice.question: Show student first names and their enrolled course names.

hints.title: Three tables
hints.content: Join students → enrollments → courses.

hints.title: ON clause
hints.content: Match student_id and course_id in the bridge table.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: 10 rows pairing student names with course names.

practice.solution: SELECT s.first_name, c.course_name FROM students s INNER JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id INNER JOIN courses c ON e.course_id = c.course_id;
practice.explanation: INNER JOIN connects students to courses through the enrollments bridge table.

summary: INNER JOIN keeps matching rows only

title: LEFT JOIN
description: Keep all rows from the left table, even without matches.
introduction: LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table. Unmatched right-side columns show NULL.

sections.title: Preserving Unmatched Rows
sections.content: LEFT JOIN is essential for finding 'missing' data — students with no enrollments, books never borrowed, etc.
sections.exampleSql: SELECT s.first_name, e.grade FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id;

practice.question: List all students and their grades. Include students with no enrollments.

hints.title: LEFT JOIN
hints.content: Use LEFT JOIN so all students appear, even without enrollments.

hints.title: NULL grades
hints.content: Frank has no enrollments — his grade will be NULL.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: More rows than students — students with multiple enrollments appear multiple times.

practice.solution: SELECT s.first_name, e.grade FROM students s LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id;
practice.explanation: LEFT JOIN preserves all left-table rows. NULL appears where no match exists.

summary: LEFT JOIN keeps all left rows

title: Subqueries & CTEs
description: Nest queries and use Common Table Expressions.
introduction: Subqueries are queries inside queries. CTEs (WITH clause) make complex queries readable.

sections.title: Queries Within Queries
sections.content: A subquery in WHERE filters based on another query's result. CTEs define temporary named results: WITH high_gpa AS (SELECT ...) SELECT * FROM high_gpa;
sections.exampleSql: WITH avg_gpa AS (SELECT AVG(gpa) AS avg FROM students WHERE gpa IS NOT NULL) SELECT * FROM students, avg_gpa WHERE students.gpa > avg_gpa.avg;

practice.question: Find students with GPA above the average GPA.

hints.title: Subquery
hints.content: Compare gpa against (SELECT AVG(gpa) FROM students WHERE gpa IS NOT NULL).

hints.title: NULL handling
hints.content: Exclude NULL GPAs from the average calculation.

hints.title: Expected
hints.content: Students with above-average GPA.

practice.solution: SELECT * FROM students WHERE gpa > (SELECT AVG(gpa) FROM students WHERE gpa IS NOT NULL);
practice.explanation: The subquery calculates average GPA. The outer query filters students above that value.

summary: Use WITH for named temporary results
